frontmatter.title: Presenting dcpos and dcpo algebras
frontmatter.meta: @article{jung-moshier-vickers-2008, author = {Achim Jung and M. Andrew Moshier and Steve Vickers}, doi = {10.1016/j.entcs.2008.10.013}, issn = {1571-0661}, journal = {Electronic Notes in Theoretical Computer Science}, keywords = {Dcpo algebras, dcpo presentations, d-topology, -topology, coverage theorems}, note = {Proceedings of the 24th Conference on the Mathematical Foundations of Programming Semantics (MFPS XXIV)}, pages = {209-229}, title = {Presenting Dcpos and Dcpo Algebras}, volume = {218}, year = {2008}, }

mainmatter.p: Dcpos can be presented by preorders of generators and inequational relations expressed as covers. Algebraic operations on the generators (possibly with their results being ideals of generators) can be extended to the dcpo presented, provided the covers are “stable” for the operations. The resulting dcpo algebra has a natural universal characterization and satisfies all the inequational laws satisfied by the generating algebra. Applications include known “coverage theorems” from locale theory.

frontmatter.title: Dcpo presentation
frontmatter.meta: Definition 2.1, presenting dcpos and dcpo algebras

mainmatter.p: A dcpo presentation consists of a preorder P of generators together with a subset C \subseteq P \times \Omega ^{ { \left \lvert P \right \rvert } } such that for every { \left ( a \lhd U \right )} \in C, the subset U is directed in P. Absent the condition that every distinguished subset be directed, we refer to C as a cover set on P.

frontmatter.title: Preserving covers from a dcpo presentation
frontmatter.meta: Definition 2.2, presenting dcpos and dcpo algebras

mainmatter.p: Let { \left ( P,C \right )} be a dcpo presentation and let A be a dcpo; a monotone map { P } \xrightarrow{{ f }} { D } is said to preserve covers in C when for all a \lhd _C U we have fa \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup _{x \in U} fx.

frontmatter.title: Sheaves relative to a cover set
frontmatter.meta: Definition 2.4, presenting dcpos and dcpo algebras

mainmatter.p: Let P be a preorder equipped with a cover set C. A C-sheaf I is defined to be a downward-closed subset of P that is closed under all covers in the sense that U \subseteq I implies a \in I for all a \lhd _C U.

frontmatter.title: The free dcpo on a dcpo presentation
frontmatter.meta: Definition 2.3, presenting dcpos and dcpo algebras

mainmatter.p: Let { \left ( P,C \right )} be a dcpo presentation, and let \bar{P} be a dcpo. A morphism of preorders { P } \xrightarrow{{ \eta }} { \bar{P} } is said to exhibit \bar{P} as the free dcpo generated by { \left ( P,C \right )} if the following conditions hold:

ol.li: every cover preserving map { P } \xrightarrow{{ f }} { A } into a dcpo A factors through { P } \xrightarrow{{ \eta }} { \bar{P} } by a unique continuous map { \bar{P} } \xrightarrow{{ \bar{f} }} { D }.

mainmatter.p: Let \mathbb{L} = { \left ( \mathsf{L} , \eta , \mu \right )} be the lifting monad on dcpos; the Eilenberg–Moore category \mathsf{dcpo} ^{ \mathbb{L} } is closed under connected colimits, and these colimits are created by the forgetful functor { \mathsf{dcpo} ^{ \mathbb{L} } } \xrightarrow{{ \mathsf{U} ^{ \mathbb{L} } }} { \mathsf{dcpo} }.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: This follows from the following facts:

ol.li: The category of dcpos is (even constructively) known to be cocomplete.
The forgetful functor { \mathsf{dcpo} ^{ \mathbb{L} } } \xrightarrow{{ \mathsf{U} ^{ \mathbb{L} } }} { \mathsf{dcpo} } creates whatever colimits \mathsf{L} preserves.
